_note: Revenue paths are built as IDENTITIES in sourced drivers, not hand-drawn fades. Each path's implied primary driver is checked against the sourced ceiling. CONTRACT §2.1 requires segment-first decomposition — this file provides the segment-level paths Daedalus needs for the SOTP.
identity_statement: Total Revenue = Σ_regions (Members_r × ARM_r) + Advertising_Revenue. Where ARM_r = subscription price per member per year in region r. Advertising_Revenue = Ad-tier MAV × (CPM × Fill_rate × Ad_load_hours). The two components are separable: subscription revenue is ARM × members; advertising is yield × reach. These two levers move at different rates and are the basis for the segment decomposition.

addressable_market_summary.note: 750M broadband HH is Netflix's own disclosed figure (ARS FY2025). 325M current subscribers = 43.3% penetration. Remaining 425M = theoretically addressable but income and competitive constraints mean realistic ceiling in 5-7 years is 400-450M. UCAN and Western EMEA are near-saturated; growth comes from LATAM penetration expansion and APAC.

segment_paths.note: All figures in USD billions. Revenue = Members × ARM / 1000 (where ARM is in USD/year). Member adds are annual net adds, not cumulative.

UCAN.driver_identity: UCAN Revenue = UCAN Members × UCAN ARM. Members are near-static (97-102M range); ARM drives growth.

base.label: UCAN Base — ARM +6%/year (2 price increases per 3 years); members flat to +1-2M/year
base.arm_growth_driver: Netflix raised UCAN standard from $15.49 to $17.99 (late 2024) and premium from $22.99 to $24.99. Historical pattern: price increase every 12-18 months. At $18-20/month standard by 2027 and $22/month by 2028, ARM reaches $216-240/year. This is consistent with inflation + value-add (live sports, ad tier expansion). Source: Netflix Q4 2025 8-K pricing disclosures; historical pricing data from Netflix shareholders letters 2019-2025.
base.ceiling_check: At $244/year ARM, UCAN ARM is $20.33/month. US Netflix standard plan is currently $17.99 and premium $24.99. A base of $20.33 average blended (including ad-tier at $7.99 base + ad ARPU supplement) is plausible. The UCAN ceiling for ARM is approximately $30/month — well above the 2028 base estimate.

bull.label: UCAN Bull — ARM +8%/year; advertising ARPU drives blended ARM above pure subscription
bull.note: Requires: (1) 2 price increases over 3 years with 8% average increment (vs. base's 6%); (2) ad-tier ARPU of ~$8-10/month (subscription $7.99 + advertising ~$8-10 yield) reaching near parity with standard ad-free plan. Evidence: Netflix's ad tech platform improving fill rates; new advanced targeting launched in 2026 enabling premium CPMs.

bear.label: UCAN Bear — ARM +4%/year; consumer pushback on price increases limits pricing power
bear.note: Requires: price increases of only 4% annually — below inflation — implying Netflix concedes on pricing to protect churn. Some churn evidence needed; no current evidence of meaningful churn response to 2024 price increases. This is a credible lower bound, not a tail.

EMEA.driver_identity: EMEA Revenue = EMEA Members × EMEA ARM. Both grow: members from lower-penetration Eastern Europe, Middle East, Africa; ARM from Western European price increases and ad tier.

base.label: EMEA Base — members +4-5M/year; ARM +5%/year
base.note: EMEA ARM of $11.49 blends Western Europe (~$13-15/month equivalent) with Eastern Europe, Middle East (~$7-9/month), and Africa (<$5/month). Price increases in UK, Germany, France are consistent with UCAN trend but smaller in magnitude due to purchasing power range. ARM growth of 5%/year is in the middle of the observed 2022-2025 EMEA ARM trajectory.

bear.label: EMEA Bear — members +3M/year; ARM +3%/year (FX drag, limited pricing in Eastern Europe)

LATAM.driver_identity: LATAM Revenue = LATAM Members × LATAM ARM. Both grow but FX creates volatility — constant-currency ARM growth is higher than reported.

base.label: LATAM Base — members +3-4M/year; ARM +5%/year constant-currency, ~3% reported (FX drag ~2%/year)
base.note: LATAM ARM growth is structurally capped by affordability. Brazil plans start at ~BRL 20-25/month (~$4-5 USD). Subscriber growth in LATAM is driven by middle-class expansion in Brazil, Mexico, Colombia. Password-sharing enforcement in 2023 was a major bolus; normalized adds are ~3-4M/year. FX drag (-2%/year reported) subdues reported ARM growth. Revenue growth of ~1-2%/year reflects modest volume + ARM growth offset by FX.

bull.label: LATAM Bull — members +5M/year; ARM +6% constant-currency; FX neutral

bear.label: LATAM Bear — members +2M/year; ARM flat (FX headwinds; no pricing power in Brazil recession scenario)
bear.note: Bear requires persistent BRL and MXN weakness plus limited subscriber adds. Consistent with a Brazil recession scenario.

APAC.driver_identity: APAC Revenue = APAC Members × APAC ARM. Fastest member growth, ARM growth constrained by India mobile-plan pricing (~$2.50-3/month) diluting the regional average.

base.label: APAC Base — members +12-15M/year; ARM +5%/year (India weight grows but higher-priced markets also add)
base.note: APAC has the largest subscriber-add runway. India has ~800M smartphone users; Netflix has modest penetration (~10-12M subs). Australia, Japan, Korea are more penetrated (~25-35%) but have higher ARPU ($8-12/month). APAC add rate of +12-15M/year is consistent with 2024-2025 trajectory (+10-14M/year actual) factoring in India growth. ARM progression from $7.34 to $8.50/month by 2028 reflects: (1) India adds diluting the average slightly, (2) offset by premium tier adoption in Japan/Korea/Australia.

bull.label: APAC Bull — India acceleration to 20-25M subs adds/year as mobile broadband expands; ARM +6%/year
bull.note: India 4G/5G penetration expanding; Reliance Jio partnership (hypothetical; not signed) could distribute Netflix at low cost. Korean/Japanese content continuing to drive EMEA and LATAM subscribers as well. APAC bull is constrained by JioCinema competition for live sports.

bear.label: APAC Bear — India growth stalls due to JioCinema competition; members +7-8M/year only
bear.note: JioCinema disrupted Netflix in India with free IPL cricket streaming. If Reliance expands free streaming to general entertainment, Netflix India growth stalls. Bear is still positive absolute growth — not a decline scenario.

Advertising.driver_identity: Ad Revenue = MAV × Annual_Ad_ARPU_per_MAV. MAV is monthly active viewers on ad-supported plans; Annual_Ad_ARPU_per_MAV = CPM × fill_rate × avg_hours_watched_per_month × 60 (minutes per hour) / 1000 (minutes per impression) × ~4 ad minutes per viewing hour.
Advertising.arithmetic_check: At $1.5B FY2025 revenue / 190M average MAV = $7.89/MAV/year = $0.66/MAV/month. This is per household equivalent. Per paying ad-plan subscriber: $1.5B / ~91M ad-plan subs = $16.5/subscriber/year = $1.37/month (subscription component excluded). For reference: YouTube generates ~$6-8/user/year in advertising on 2.7B MAUs — Netflix's ad yield per MAV should eventually exceed YouTube's because (1) longer average viewing sessions, (2) premium-quality content commands higher CPMs, (3) fewer users (supply scarcity vs. YouTube's ubiquity).

base.label: Ad Base — revenue reaches $6.5B by 2028 as fill rates improve and MAV scales to 300M+ (WARC 9.2% CTV share by 2027 pathway)
base.ceiling_check: At $6.5B ad revenue on 325M MAV = $20/MAV/year. Global CTV market $46.9B in 2028 × Netflix 14% share = $6.6B. Consistent — requires 14% CTV share, above WARC's 9.2% (2027) but plausible for 2028. This is WITHIN the ceiling. Base passes ceiling check.
base.note: FY2026: $3B guided (company filed, HIGH confidence). FY2027: $5B = $3B growing at ~65% (decelerating from 100%). FY2028: $6.5B = $5B growing at 30%. The deceleration curve is deliberate: fill rates improve quickly in years 1-3 then plateau; CPMs moderate as market matures. By 2028 the revenue is based on a more stable yield, not just volume growth.

bull.label: Ad Bull — revenue reaches $9.5B by 2028, ahead of the 2030 internal target
bull.ceiling_check: At $9.5B on 340M MAV = $27.9/MAV/year. Global CTV $46.9B × Netflix 20% = $9.4B — at the ceiling of plausible CTV share. Bull is AT the ceiling of the CTV market — it requires Netflix capturing 20% of global CTV ad spend by 2028 vs. WARC's 9.2% forecast for 2027. Ambitious but arithmetically possible if non-CTV international ad markets (less measured) contribute. This is a CEILING-ADJACENT bull, not comfortable headroom. Flag: stacked positive assumptions required.
bull.note: Bull requires: in-house ad tech delivering YouTube-level fill rates (85%+) by 2027; premium CPMs from live sports inventory; advanced targeting driving advertiser ROAS premium. All three mechanisms have named product initiatives but are NOT contracted.

bear.label: Ad Bear — revenue reaches only $4.5B by 2028 as Amazon CPM competition and fill-rate plateau suppress yield
bear.ceiling_check: At $4.5B on 300M MAV = $15/MAV/year. Global CTV $46.9B × Netflix 9.6% = $4.5B. Consistent with WARC's ~9.2% trajectory for 2027 extending modestly to 2028. WELL WITHIN the ceiling. Bear passes ceiling check with room.
bear.note: Bear requires: Amazon's first-party purchase data enables structurally higher CPMs on Prime Video, pulling advertiser budgets from Netflix; fill rates stall at 55-65% due to limited programmatic demand in non-US markets; CPM decline from early-market premium normalization. This is OBSERVABLE AND NEAR-TERM — Amazon's ad product is scaling now.

total_revenue_paths.note: Sum of four subscription regions plus advertising. Members are total paying subscribers (subscription revenue denominator). Ad revenue is a separate line.

base.fy2027_calc: UCAN $23.0 + EMEA $16.3 + LATAM $5.4 + APAC $7.9 + Advertising $5.0 + Other $0.9 = ~$58.5B
base.fy2028_calc: UCAN $24.6 + EMEA $17.8 + LATAM $5.8 + APAC $9.6 + Advertising $6.5 + Other $2.0 = ~$66.3B
base.implied_primary_driver_check: 2028 implied total ARM = $163/year on 367M subscribers. Subscription ARM growth from $140 to $163 = +16.4% cumulative over 3 years = +5.2%/year blended (includes mix shift to lower-ARPU APAC subs). Ad revenue $6.5B = $17.7/subscriber/year additional. Total yield: $163 + $17.7 = $180.7/subscriber/year, up from $139 in FY2025 = +9.0%/year compound. This DOES NOT exceed any sourced ceiling — global SVOD market alone is ~$98B in 2026 growing to ~$130B by 2028; Netflix's $60B subscription revenue = 46% of that market. Consistent.

bull.note: UCAN $27.3 + EMEA $19.6 + LATAM $6.7 + APAC $11.6 + Advertising $9.5 - rounding = ~$74.7B. Caps at $74B. Bull requires: 8%/year subscription ARM in UCAN/EMEA, 20% CTV share capture, APAC adds of 15-20M/year.
bull.ceiling_check: $74B subscription revenue = ~$76% of 2028 projected global SVOD market (~$98B today growing at 12%/year to ~$137B by 2028). Plausible — Netflix would hold ~46% of a larger SVOD market, same penetration as today. PASSES ceiling check.

bear.note: UCAN $23.0 + EMEA $16.2 + LATAM $4.8 + APAC $7.6 + Advertising $4.5 + Other $1.4 = ~$57.5B. Bear reflects: APAC disappointment, LATAM FX drag, advertising yield suppression from Amazon competition. Still positive growth — Netflix's subscription business has a durable floor.
bear.ceiling_check: Below base; well within all market ceilings. PASSES ceiling check.

operating_margin_paths.note: Content spend $18B guided flat 2026. Revenue growing ~13% (base) means content as % of revenue falls from ~40% (FY2025) to ~27% (FY2028 base). Operating margin expansion follows. Technology, G&A, and marketing scale more slowly than revenue.

content_spend_assumptions.note: Base: content grows $1B/year from 2026. At $20B content on $66B revenue = 30.3% of revenue vs 40% today. Remainder of P&L (tech, G&A, mktg) scales at ~3-4%/year absolute. Operating income: $66.3B × 35.5% = ~$23.5B base FY2028.

fcf_paths.note: FCF = Operating income + content amortization - content additions - capex. Netflix's content is a cash-heavy business: additions ($16-18B) > amortization ($15-17B) in growth years. The gap narrows as content slate matures. Content cash outflow: net ~$0.7-1.0B/year above P&L charge.

normalization_year_assessment.rationale: Netflix's high-growth phase (15-20%+ revenue growth) is transitioning to a mid-growth phase (10-14%) by 2027-2028. By 2030, subscriber adds in APAC and LATAM will have normalized, advertising revenue will have reached a more mature growth rate (15-20%/year from a larger base), and content spend will have found a steady-state ratio to revenue. At normalization (~2029-2030), we estimate: revenue ~$80-90B (base), operating margin ~36-38%, FCF ~$25-30B. These are the normalization-year earnings Daedalus should apply a steady-state multiple to.
normalization_year_assessment.note: The exit-multiple method (METHODS.md) requires normalization when g < r. At 8% growth vs. ~9% WACC, this is just entering well-behaved territory by 2030.

data_gaps: Netflix's own segment operating margins are not disclosed — content spend is reported at company level, not by region. Segment EBIT is not available. Daedalus will need to use blended margin with regional revenue weight.

Ad-tier ARPU decomposition (subscription component vs. advertising component) is not separately disclosed by Netflix. The $1.37/month implied advertising supplement per ad-plan subscriber is inferred, not company-stated.
APAC subscriber count by country (India vs. Japan vs. Korea vs. Rest) is not disclosed, making India-specific thesis validation difficult.
Cash tax rate (vs. GAAP provision) requires 10-K tax footnote analysis not completed in this bundle.
The 'per ad-tier user' revenue figure ($70.44 declining to $59.67) is single-source (IMDB-cited); exact source not independently verified.

sources.name: Netflix Q4 2025 8-K — FY2026 guidance revenue $50.7-51.7B, FCF $12.5B, content spend $18B, ad revenue $3B
Netflix Q1 2026 8-K — 325M subscribers, segments by region, Q2 guidance $11.8B
Netflix Annual Report FY2025 — 750M+ broadband HH, $650B+ entertainment revenue pool
MNTN Research: CTV ad spend $46.89B by 2028
WARC: Netflix 9.2% CTV ad share by 2027
Netflix ad tier 250M MAV May 2026 (up from 190M Nov 2025)
Netflix $9B ad revenue internal 2030 target (management commentary); Goldman Sachs $9.5B
Omdia: online video subscription revenue $174B (2025) to $216B (2030)
Netflix India APAC strategy — Variety APOS 2026 report
